note.soap:
S:  
34-year-old patient reports a tick bite sustained in Georgia with the tick attached for approximately 37 hours. The patient notes an expanding bullseye-like rash consistent with erythema migrans. No systemic symptoms such as fever, headache, neck stiffness, facial palsy, dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or confusion are reported.

O:  
No physical exam findings available due to phone-only encounter. History is notable for erythema migrans-like rash at the site of tick bite and absence of systemic symptoms.

A:  
- Working diagnosis (most likely): Early localized Lyme disease (erythema migrans)  
- Differential: Localized tick bite reaction, cellulitis, Southern tick-associated rash illness (STARI)  
- Red flags considered: No severe headache or stiff neck, no neurological deficits or facial palsy, no cardiopulmonary symptoms, no high fever or confusion, no widespread rash

P:  
- Disposition: Patient appropriate for outpatient management with close follow-up  
- Plan: Empiric treatment for early localized Lyme disease may be appropriate; reviewing physician will confirm and prescribe if indicated. Educate patient on completing full course of antibiotics if prescribed. Advise monitoring for development of systemic symptoms or progression of rash.  
- Safety net: Return promptly if patient develops any of the following: severe headache or neck stiffness, new neurological symptoms (facial palsy, weakness), chest pain, palpitations, syncope, shortness of breath, high fever, confusion, or if rash spreads significantly or worsens.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.
